Just now

Image Generated by AIGoogle isn’t replacing Retrieval-Augmented Generation. It might just be making it smarter.
Every few months, the AI industry introduces a new concept that sparks endless discussions. A while ago, it was prompt engineering. Then came Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), followed by AI agents, Model Context Protocol (MCP), and multi-agent systems. Now, another term has started appearing across LinkedIn, X, and developer communities: Google’s Open Knowledge Format (OKF).
Almost immediately, headlines began appearing claiming that “RAG is dead” or that vector databases would soon become obsolete. Like many people, I was curious. But instead of jumping to conclusions, I decided to understand what OKF actually is, why Google introduced it, and whether it truly changes how we build AI applications.
After spending time exploring the concept, I came away with a very different conclusion. I don’t think OKF replaces RAG.
I think it makes RAG better.
The Real Problem Isn’t Retrieval. It’s Knowledge Organization.
Over the last two years, we’ve spent a lot of time improving AI models. We’ve experimented with better prompts, more capable language models…
